anatomy: Pectoralis major has two heads — the clavicular (upper) and the larger sternocostal (mid/lower) — plus the small pec minor beneath.
function: Horizontal adduction — bring the arm across the front of the body
Shoulder flexion — the upper head raises the arm forward
Internal rotation of the humerus
uses: Any pushing movement — a door, a heavy object, a person off you
Throwing and swinging actions across the body

ex.how: Stable, deep stretch, dead-easy to progress — Nippard's overall #1.
Sitting removes balance; constant tension + big stretch.
30–45° bench; deep stretch, biases the upper chest.
Deeper bottom stretch than a barbell.
Best overload potential; slightly less stretch than DBs.
Upper-chest bias, heavy and loadable.
Lean torso forward to shift tension onto the chest.
Fixed path flye — very easy to feel the pec.
Set pulley high→low to bias different regions.
Scalable pressing; add plate or elevate feet.
Hits chest a little — but it's really a lat exercise.
Kills the stretch; tension leaks to triceps — Nippard's worst.
Bar to the neck — risk isn't worth it.

anatomy: The anterior head of the deltoid, on the front of the shoulder.
function: Shoulder flexion — raise the arm forward and overhead
Assists horizontal adduction and pressing
uses: Reaching and lifting in front of you
Already heavily worked in all bench and overhead pressing

ex.how: Deep stretch, low skill — Nippard's #1 for front delts (A+).
Full range, honest stretch at the bottom.
Heavy, but more systemic than delt-specific (B+).
Between flat and vertical; upper-chest/front-delt.
Redundant — front delts are already smashed by all pressing.

anatomy: The lateral (middle) head of the deltoid — the cap that gives shoulders width.
function: Shoulder abduction — raise the arm out to the side
Creates the 3D, wide-shoulder look
uses: Lifting and carrying things out to the side

ex.how: Cable behind the body matches the strength curve and stretches the side delt — Nippard's #1.
Constant tension through a big arc.
Loads the stretched position hard.
Lean away from the weight; tension deeper into the stretch.
Atlantis-style standing machine — smooth, loadable.
Solid, but standing versions load the stretch better.
DBs from hips to overhead; long arc, light load.
Works the side delt + traps; watch cranky shoulders.

anatomy: The posterior head of the deltoid, on the back of the shoulder.
function: Horizontal abduction — pull the arm back and out
External rotation; scapular control and posture
uses: Balancing all the pressing you do

ex.how: Even tension across the full arc — Nippard's #1 for rear delts.
Excellent tension + stretch, no setup fuss.
Pull to the face, elbows high — also great for shoulder health.
Hinge over, flare the elbows out.
Bench removes momentum.

anatomy: Latissimus dorsi — the large fan from the upper arm down to the lower back that creates back width.
function: Shoulder adduction — pull the arm down toward the body
Shoulder extension — pull the arm behind you
Internal rotation; assists pulling the body up

ex.how: Great stretch + smooth tension + easy progression.
Comfortable joints, strong pull — Tier 1.
Long stretch, unilateral focus.
Nippard's best all-around back move — no momentum.
Row to the sternum, control the stretch forward.
Landmine unilateral row; huge stretch at the bottom.
Add load once bodyweight is easy.
Big loadable row; keep the torso still.
Deep stretch that isolates the lat.
Underhand; lats + biceps (Tier 3).
Loadable, but stability limits it vs chest-supported.
Pure shoulder extension, great mind-muscle.
Back works only isometrically — better for erectors.
Too much balancing, too little tension — Nippard's worst.

anatomy: Trapezius — a large diamond with three parts: upper (neck to shoulder), middle, and lower.
function: Upper: elevate the shoulder blades (shrug)
Middle: retract the shoulder blades together
Lower: depress/pull the shoulder blades down
uses: Upright posture and a strong-looking neckline

ex.how: Shrug back at the top, stretch at the bottom — Nippard favourite.
Massive upper-trap tension in the overhead position.
Elevate straight up, pause hard at the top.
Heaviest loadable shrug.
Hits mid/lower traps + rear delts.

anatomy: Rhomboids and mid/lower traps — the muscles between the shoulder blades.
function: Scapular retraction — squeeze the shoulder blades together
Keeps the shoulder blades set and the chest open
Posture and pulling power
uses: Bracing the upper back under load

ex.how: Retract and squeeze the blades at the top — Nippard's best back move.
Drive elbows back, pinch the blades.
Unilateral landmine row; strong mid-back squeeze.
Retraction + external rotation for posture.
Elbows flared to bias the upper back.

anatomy: Biceps brachii (long + short head) plus the brachialis underneath, which pushes the biceps up.
function: Elbow flexion — bend the arm
Forearm supination — rotate the palm up
Slight shoulder flexion (long head)
uses: Arm size and the biceps peak

ex.how: Arms behind the torso — max stretch, even tension. Nippard #1.
Angled pad crushes the stretched bottom position.
Arm hangs behind the body; loads the stretch.
Constant tension, easy to progress.
Neutral grip; builds brachialis + arm thickness/peak.
Great overload, but uneven tension + shallow stretch.
Elbow braced; strong squeeze.
Simple, supinate as you lift.
Nippard groups it with the curls to skip.
Awkward wrist strain, poor loading — Nippard's worst.

anatomy: Triceps brachii — long, lateral and medial heads. The long head crosses the shoulder, so it grows best stretched overhead.
function: Elbow extension — straighten the arm
Long head assists shoulder extension/adduction
uses: Throwing, punching, pushing up off the floor

ex.how: Long-head stretch; ~40% more growth than pressdowns in studies.
Same stretch, freer wrist path.
Simple, easy to overload — near S but weaker long-head.
Constant tension into the shortened position.
Lower behind the head for a bigger stretch.
Loadable compound for the triceps.
Stay upright to bias the triceps.
Spread the rope at the bottom.
Fine, but options above beat it.
No tension on the negative — Nippard's last pick.

anatomy: Wrist flexors (inner) and extensors (outer), plus the brachioradialis running to the elbow.
function: Wrist flexion and extension
Grip — closing and holding
Elbow flexion (brachioradialis)
uses: Grip strength for every pull and carry

ex.how: Flexors; let the bar roll to the fingers for full range.
Extensors; light and controlled.
Great flexor stretch off the glutes.
Brachioradialis + extensors; overhand grip.
Neutral grip loads brachioradialis.
Grip endurance under real load.

anatomy: Quadriceps — four heads (rectus femoris + three vasti). Rectus femoris also crosses the hip.
function: Knee extension — straighten the leg
Hip flexion (rectus femoris)
Absorbing landing forces

ex.how: Deep knee bend under load — 'a brilliant quad builder.'
Arc keeps constant tension on the quads.
King compound; huge loadable quad stimulus.
Fixed path lets you sit into the quads, feet forward.
Unilateral; big stretch on the front leg.
Upright torso biases the quads.
Near-perfect isolation; add lengthened partials.
Bodyweight quad stretch under tension.
Long stride, controlled descent.
Beginner-friendly; caps out on load.
Loadable but shallower quad stretch than the hack.
Instability steals tension from the quads.

anatomy: Three muscles crossing both the hip and knee — so they both extend the hip and flex the knee.
function: Knee flexion — bend the knee
Hip extension — drive the hips forward
Decelerating the leg when sprinting

ex.how: Hip hinge with a big loadable stretch — Nippard #1 if only one.
Knee flexion with the hip bent = stretched hamstring.
Isolation; squeeze and control the lower.
Even bigger stretch than the RDL.
Brutal eccentric knee flexion.
Barbell hip hinge; keep it light and clean.
Round the back slightly, drive from the hips.

anatomy: Gluteus maximus (the big power muscle) plus medius and minimus on the side of the hip.
function: Hip extension — drive the hips forward
Hip abduction — move the leg out (medius/minimus)
External rotation; pelvic stability

ex.how: Hits upper, mid AND lower glute — Nippard's surprise #1.
Round the upper back, squeeze at the top.
Extra depth = extra glute stretch.
Directly loads the upper glute (medius/minimus).
Torso lean forward for more glute.
Hip hinge hits glutes + hamstrings.
Popular, but awkward loading & comfort cap it at high-B — not S.
Isolation; squeeze at full hip extension.

ex.n: Donkey kick / fire hydrant / KB swing
ex.how: Hard to overload, little tension in the stretch.

anatomy: Gastrocnemius (the visible two-headed muscle, crosses the knee) and the deeper soleus (doesn't cross the knee).
function: Ankle plantarflexion — point the toes / rise on the balls of the feet
Propulsion in walking, running, jumping
Ankle stability

ex.how: Straight leg = gastrocnemius; full stretch, pause at the bottom.
Bent knee = soleus; slow, deep reps.
Loadable; push through the balls of the feet.
Fixed path, easy to overload.

anatomy: Rectus abdominis (the six-pack), obliques on the sides, and deep transverse abdominis that braces.
function: Spinal flexion — crunch the ribs toward the hips
Anti-extension, anti-rotation, anti-lateral-flexion (bracing)
Transfers force between upper and lower body

ex.how: Loadable spinal flexion; round the spine, don't just hip-hinge.
Curl the pelvis up — full-range lower abs.
Whole-core control; lower slowly, body rigid.
Anti-rotation; resist the cable twisting you.
Anti-lateral-flexion; walk tall, one-sided load.
Extra range and load over a flat crunch.
Rotation for the obliques; slow and deliberate.
Anti-extension; brace hard, add time/load.

anatomy: Erector spinae — the columns running up either side of the spine, plus the thoracolumbar fascia.
function: Spinal extension — straighten and arch the back
Anti-flexion — keep the spine rigid under load
Keeps you upright and stable
uses: Keeping a neutral spine when lifting
Standing and sitting upright all day

ex.how: Loadable spinal + hip extension; controlled range.
Erectors work hard isometrically to hold position.
Full posterior-chain builder; keep the spine neutral.
Gentle loaded extension, easy on the spine.
Low-load endurance and control.
